And then so how do you use them? So one useful thing for you to build for yourself, by the way, for those of you who like checklist, is this. Is your personal asset list? Is what are all of the things that you own? And many of them, interestingly, are things people would not normally think of as assets. And I just pulled a few, two pages of this, that I work off of. So like missing from here, that I didn't put on here is say alcoholism, which most people would not think of as an asset. It's on my asset list. Huge percentage of the population has had or has a drinking problem. It's a wide swath of connection. Everybody has somebody in their family who has a drinking problem. It's a wide swath of connection. It's not a narrow swath of connection. Now most people would not think of it as an asset. And it's not when you currently have it. But when it's past, then it belongs on the asset list. So I gave you an example of an asset list to kind of help you start thinking. And so the exercise is to build your personal narrative asset list to get at least 10 or 15 or 20 items on it. And to come up with a story that you are not now using. So basically what I discovered is when I was making my asset list is that I do have a lot of childhood stories. So instead of moving forward with things that my customers can't relate to, there's a lot of childhood stories, strife between my father and I, and then the bond between my grandfather and I, and it's the same rich dad, poor dad thing. My father was very, very wealthy, successful medical doctor, but when I think in back, when I was like 10 years old, he backed down from a fight and from another father, and in my mind he was weak. Meanwhile, I have my crazy ex-mob enforcer, World War II Marine grandfather, who's carrying blackjacks and machetes in his car. And so there's a lot of stories that I haven't tapped.
